text:
FOUNDATION MEDICINE, INC.
FOUNDATIONONE CDx COMPREHENSIVE GENOMIC PROFILING REPORT

--- PATIENT & SPECIMEN INFORMATION ---
Patient Name: Gerald Hutchinson
Date of Birth: [on file]
Sex: Male
Ordering Physician: [GU Oncology Attending]
Institution: [Academic Medical Center GU Oncology]
Clinical Diagnosis: Castration-resistant prostate adenocarcinoma (mCRPC); germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant known
Specimen ID: FM-PRCA-[XXXXXXXX]
Specimen Type: Formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue -- prostate needle core biopsy (re-biopsy at time of castration resistance)
Biopsy Site: Prostate (primary site re-biopsy, transrectal ultrasound-guided, left base)
Date of Procedure: [see clinical record]
Tumor Nuclei Percentage (Pathologist Estimate): 60%
Sequencing Platform: Hybrid-capture based next-generation sequencing; all classes of genomic alterations assessed
Genes Analyzed: 324 cancer-related genes (full coding sequence), select introns for rearrangement detection, whole-genome copy number analysis
Mean Coverage Depth: 724x (tumor); 471x (matched blood normal)
FDA-Approved Indication: FoundationOne CDx is an FDA-approved companion diagnostic for multiple tumor types including prostate cancer
Quality Control: PASS

--- GENOMIC FINDINGS SUMMARY ---

SHORT VARIANTS (SUBSTITUTIONS / INSERTIONS / DELETIONS)

1. BRCA2 p.Ser1982Argfs*22 (c.5946delT)
   Variant Class: Short variant -- frameshift deletion
   Allele Frequency: 93%
   Classification: PATHOGENIC
   Notes: This frameshift variant results in a premature stop codon at amino acid position 2003, predicting loss of the C-terminal BRCA2 functional domain including the RAD51-binding repeats (BRC repeats 5-8) and the C-terminal DNA binding domain. This variant is concordant with the germline pathogenic variant previously identified on Myriad myRisk testing (germline BRCA2 c.5946delT). Loss of heterozygosity (LOH) is confirmed at this locus by copy number analysis (see below), establishing biallelic inactivation. This finding is consistent with the "two-hit" model of tumor suppressor inactivation and constitutes a somatic LOH event at the BRCA2 locus in the context of a germline heterozygous carrier. BRCA2 biallelic loss confers sensitivity to PARP inhibitors (ola, Rubraca; FDA-approved for mCRPC) and platinum-based chemotherapy.

2. TP53 p.R175H (c.524G>A)
   Variant Class: Short variant -- missense substitution
   Allele Frequency: 48%
   Classification: PATHOGENIC
   Notes: R175H is among the most common and well-characterized gain-of-function TP53 hotspot mutations in human cancer. Arginine-to-histidine substitution at codon 175 disrupts the DNA-contact and structural integrity of the p53 protein, abrogating its function as a tumor suppressor and conferring oncogenic gain-of-function activities including promotion of invasion, drug resistance, and altered metabolism. This mutation is present at ~50% allele frequency, consistent with hemizygous loss (one allele mutated, second allele subject to LOH on the 17p arm). In prostate cancer, TP53 alteration is associated with more aggressive disease biology and resistance to AR-directed therapies. No co-occurring MDM2 amplification detected.

COPY NUMBER ALTERATIONS (CNAs)

3. AR Amplification
   Locus: Xq12
   Copy Number: 8 copies (baseline diploid reference)
   Classification: AMPLIFICATION
   Notes: Androgen receptor gene amplification is among the most common resistance mechanisms in CRPC, detected in approximately 30-60% of CRPC specimens. At 8 copies, this represents high-level amplification. AR amplification leads to elevated AR protein expression, enabling sustained AR signaling even at castrate testosterone levels, effectively bypassing hormonal suppression. This mechanism underlies resistance to androgen deprivation therapy and is associated with reduced benefit from second-generation AR-signaling inhibitors (Xtandi, abi). Importantly, no AR point mutations conferring ligand-binding domain alterations (T878A, W742C, F877L, L702H) were detected in this specimen. The amplification in isolation, without a sensitizing AR mutation, may indicate that AR pathway activity is maintained through overexpression rather than altered ligand specificity.

4. RB1 Deep Deletion
   Locus: 13q14.2
   Copy Number: 0 (homozygous deletion)
   Classification: LOSS -- DEEP DELETION
   Notes: Homozygous deletion of the RB1 tumor suppressor locus. RB1 encodes the retinoblastoma protein, a critical cell cycle checkpoint regulator at the G1/S boundary. Biallelic RB1 loss is associated with aggressive prostate cancer biology including accelerated cell cycle progression, resistance to AR-pathway inhibitors, and phenotypic plasticity -- including increased risk of neuroendocrine or small cell transformation over time. The absence of neuroendocrine differentiation on the current biopsy (synaptophysin, chromogranin, INSM1 not assessed on this NGS specimen but negative on prior TRUS biopsy IHC) is noted; however, RB1 loss is a genomic risk factor for this transformation and warrants monitoring.

5. PTEN Homozygous Deletion
   Locus: 10q23.31
   Copy Number: 0 (homozygous deletion)
   Classification: LOSS -- DEEP DELETION
   Notes: Biallelic loss of the PTEN tumor suppressor gene. PTEN is the major negative regulator of the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling pathway; its loss results in constitutive activation of this pro-survival axis. PTEN loss occurs in approximately 20-30% of localized prostate cancers and in up to 40-50% of CRPC specimens. Combined PTEN loss and AR amplification represent co-occurring alterations that together drive robust tumor cell survival through parallel signaling pathways (AR axis and PI3K/AKT). Note: PIK3CA mutation was not detected (see below); PTEN loss is the primary PI3K-pathway activation mechanism in this tumor. PTEN loss is associated with aggressive disease and reduced benefit from AR-pathway inhibition.

6. MYC Amplification
   Locus: 8q24.21
   Copy Number: 4 copies (low-level amplification / gain)
   Classification: AMPLIFICATION (LOW-LEVEL)
   Notes: Low-level MYC amplification at 8q24, a common copy number event in prostate cancer occurring at increased frequency in CRPC. MYC drives global transcriptional amplification, metabolic reprogramming, and cell cycle progression. In the context of co-occurring AR amplification, MYC gain may cooperate to sustain tumor cell proliferation.

7. CDK4 Copy Number Gain
   Locus: 12q14.1
   Copy Number: 3 copies (low-level gain)
   Classification: GAIN
   Notes: Low-level CDK4 gain. CDK4 is a cell cycle kinase that, in complex with cyclin D, phosphorylates and inactivates RB1 to promote S-phase entry. In this tumor, CDK4 gain combined with deep RB1 deletion creates a context where the canonical CDK4-mediated growth suppressive checkpoint is entirely abrogated (RB1 protein absent). CDK4/6 inhibitors (Ibrance, ribo, abema) have limited rationale in the setting of biallelic RB1 loss.

GENES WITH NO PATHOGENIC ALTERATION DETECTED (SELECTED)

- CDK12: No mutation detected (no biallelic CDK12 loss or deleterious mutation)
- PIK3CA: No mutation detected
- SPOP: No mutation detected
- AR (point mutations): T878A, W742C, F877L, L702H -- not detected
- CDH1: No mutation detected
- ATM: No somatic mutation detected (germline result: no pathogenic variant per prior testing)
- BRCA1: No somatic mutation detected (germline: no pathogenic variant)
- PALB2: No somatic mutation detected
- IDH1/IDH2: No mutation detected
- FGFR1/FGFR2/FGFR3: No amplification or mutation detected
- WNT pathway (APC, CTNNB1): No pathogenic alteration
- MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2: No somatic pathogenic alterations detected

REARRANGEMENTS / FUSIONS
- No gene fusions or rearrangements detected in queried intron regions (including TMPRSS2-ERG, RAF kinase fusions, NTRK fusions).
- Note: TMPRSS2-ERG fusion not detected; ETS family fusion status: negative by this assay.

--- GENOMIC BIOMARKERS ---

TUMOR MUTATIONAL BURDEN (TMB)
  Result: 4 mutations/Mb (LOW)
  Interpretation: Below the threshold for TMB-High designation (>=10 mutations/Mb). TMB-Low status indicates that immune checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy based solely on TMB is unlikely to confer clinical benefit. This is concordant with the microsatellite stability finding.

MICROSATELLITE INSTABILITY (MSI)
  Result: MICROSATELLITE STABLE (MSS)
  Interpretation: No evidence of mismatch repair deficiency (dMMR) by MSI analysis. This is consistent with the absence of pathogenic somatic variants in MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, and PMS2, and with prior germline testing showing no Lynch syndrome-associated pathogenic variants. pembro (Keytruda) based on MSI-H/dMMR indication is not applicable.

GENOMIC LOSS OF HETEROZYGOSITY (gLOH)
  Result: 18% (ELEVATED)
  Threshold for HRD-High designation: >= 16%
  Interpretation: ELEVATED / HRD-HIGH
  Notes: Genomic loss of heterozygosity at 18% exceeds the validated threshold for homologous recombination deficiency (HRD). This is biologically consistent with the identified biallelic BRCA2 loss, which is the likely driver of genome instability manifesting as elevated gLOH (genomic scarring). Elevated gLOH is independently associated with clinical benefit from PARP inhibitors (ola per PROfound trial, ruca per TRITON2/3 data) and is a companion biomarker for PARP inhibitor eligibility in mCRPC. The elevated gLOH further supports the pathobiological significance of the BRCA2 biallelic loss in this tumor.

--- ACTIONABLE ALTERATIONS AND CLINICAL ANNOTATIONS ---

TIER I -- FDA-APPROVED THERAPIES WITH COMPANION DIAGNOSTIC INDICATION IN THIS TUMOR TYPE:

  BRCA2 biallelic loss (germline + somatic LOH)
  - ola (Lynparza, AstraZeneca/MSD): FDA-approved for mCRPC in patients with deleterious or suspected deleterious germline or somatic BRCA1/2 mutations, based on PROfound trial. FoundationOne CDx is the FDA-approved companion diagnostic for this indication. APPLICABLE.
  - Rubraca (Rubraca, Clovis Oncology): FDA-approved for mCRPC in patients with BRCA1/2 alteration (accelerated approval, based on TRITON2). APPLICABLE.
  Note: ola is preferred over Rubraca based on more mature PROfound data and broader clinical adoption.

TIER II -- LIKELY CLINICALLY RELEVANT / POTENTIAL RESISTANCE MECHANISMS:

  AR Amplification (8x)
  - High-level AR amplification is an established mechanism of resistance to AR-signaling inhibitors (Xtandi, Zytiga, darolutamide, Erleada). Presence of AR amplification at CRPC transition is consistent with selection during prior ADT plus Zytiga and is expected to confer reduced efficacy of enza or other ARSI as subsequent therapy.
  - No AR ligand binding domain point mutations detected; resistance mechanism appears to be amplification-driven overexpression rather than altered ligand specificity.

  PTEN Loss (homozygous deletion) + PI3K/AKT pathway activation
  - Combined PTEN loss and AR amplification: PTEN loss may predict primary or secondary resistance to AR-pathway inhibitors. In the context of mCRPC, PTEN loss has been associated with reduced rPFS on Xtandi (AFFIRM/PREVAIL subset analyses). AKT inhibitors (ipatasertib, capivasertib) in combination with Xtandi have been evaluated in mCRPC with PTEN loss (IPATential150 trial); capivasertib plus Xtandi (CAPItello-281) is an emerging therapeutic option for PTEN-deficient CRPC.
  - PTEN/AKT pathway: no currently FDA-approved AKT inhibitor for prostate cancer at time of this report, but clinical trial eligibility should be considered.

  RB1 Loss (homozygous deletion)
  - Biallelic RB1 loss is associated with AR pathway independence, cell-cycle dysregulation, and increased risk of lineage plasticity / neuroendocrine transformation over time.
  - Clinical implication: heightened surveillance for neuroendocrine differentiation on subsequent biopsies and/or serum markers (chromogranin A, NSE). Platinum-VP-16 chemotherapy may be appropriate if neuroendocrine transformation occurs.
  - CDK4/6 inhibitors: NOT recommended given biallelic RB1 loss (loss of the drug target's downstream effector).

  TP53 p.R175H (gain-of-function)
  - Gain-of-function TP53 mutation associated with chemotherapy resistance and aggressive tumor biology. No currently FDA-approved targeted therapy directed at p53 R175H in prostate cancer. Clinical trial enrollment including p53-directed strategies (APR-246/eprenetapopt, PRIMA-1Met) may be considered in later lines.

TIER III -- BIOMARKERS WITHOUT CURRENTLY MATCHED THERAPY (INVESTIGATIONAL CONTEXT):
  - MYC amplification: No direct therapeutic target; relevant as a marker of aggressive biology.
  - CDK4 gain: CDK4/6 inhibitor therapy not applicable given co-occurring biallelic RB1 loss.

--- CLINICAL TRIAL MATCHING ---
Based on identified alterations, the patient may be eligible for the following categories of trials (not exhaustive; recommend institutional clinical trials office consultation):
  - PARP inhibitor combination trials (e.g., PARP + AR-pathway inhibitor combinations for BRCA2 mCRPC after prior ARSI)
  - AKT inhibitor combination trials for PTEN-loss mCRPC
  - DNA damage repair (DDR) pathway trials for BRCA2/HRD-positive mCRPC
  - Novel AR-degrader (PROTAC) trials for AR-amplified/AR-overexpressing CRPC
  - Lutetium-177 PSMA-617 trials (PSMA expression not assessed by this assay; recommend PSMA PET/CT for eligibility assessment)

--- PATHOLOGY CORRELATION NOTE ---
The molecular profile of this tumor is consistent with high-grade, treatment-experienced CRPC. The combination of BRCA2 biallelic loss, AR amplification, TP53 gain-of-function mutation, RB1 loss, and PTEN deletion represents a convergence of major resistance-conferring alterations that drive castration resistance and limit efficacy of AR-directed therapy. No neuroendocrine differentiation markers are assessed on this NGS assay; clinical and histological monitoring for neuroendocrine transformation is warranted given the RB1 and TP53 co-alteration context ("TP53/RB1 double loss" phenotype associated with lineage plasticity in prostate cancer). This report is intended for use by qualified healthcare professionals in conjunction with clinical context, histopathology, and relevant guidelines.

--- REPORT ATTESTATION ---
This report was generated by Foundation Medicine, Inc. (Cambridge, MA) using FoundationOne CDx. FoundationOne CDx is FDA-approved as a companion diagnostic and is regulated as an in vitro diagnostic device. All identified alterations have been reviewed and classified by qualified molecular pathologists. This report does not constitute a recommendation for a specific treatment. Clinical decisions should be made in conjunction with the treating physician, multidisciplinary team input, and applicable clinical guidelines (NCCN, EAU).

Molecular Pathologist (Electronic Signature): [Foundation Medicine Staff Pathologist, MD/PhD]
CAP/CLIA Accredited Laboratory: Foundation Medicine, Inc. -- CLIA #22D2027531

--- END OF REPORT ---
